findings.message: N+1 query pattern: the endpoint first fetches all item ids, then issues one SELECT per id via session.get(Item, iid) inside the loop. On a table with N rows this performs N+1 round-trips to the database, scaling linearly with row count on an unbounded input (no pagination/limit). Under concurrent load this also amplifies connection-pool pressure and tail latency.
findings.suggestion: Fetch the names in a single query, e.g. `names = list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`, and drop the id-then-get loop entirely. Also add pagination (limit/offset or keyset) since the result set is unbounded.

findings.message: Unbounded result set: get_item_names returns every row's name with no LIMIT/pagination. As the items table grows this becomes a latent O(N) memory + payload regression and a denial-of-service amplifier independent of the N+1 issue.
findings.suggestion: Add a `limit`/`offset` (or keyset cursor) query parameter with a sane default cap, and apply it to the SELECT.

findings.message: Function falls off the end without `return names`, so it implicitly returns None and FastAPI's `response_model=list[str]` validation will fail at request time. Not strictly a performance finding, but it means the hot path is currently broken before the N+1 cost is even paid.
findings.suggestion: Add `return names` (or, better, return the result of the single-query rewrite above).
